author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew II. 16.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Then Herod, when he saw that he was mocked of the wise men, was exceeding wroth.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yet surely it was a case not for anger, but for fear and awe: he ought to have perceived that he was attempting impossible things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But he is not refrained.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when a soul is insensible and incurable, it yields to none of the medicines given by God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See for example this man following up his former efforts,and adding many murders to one, and hurried down the steep any whither.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For driven wild by this anger, and envy, as by some demon, he takes account of nothing, but rages even against nature herself, and his anger against the wise men who had mocked him he vents upon the children that had done no wrong: venturing then in Palestine upon a deed akin to the things that had been done in Egypt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he “sent forth,” it is said, “and slew all the children that were in Bethlehem, and in all the coasts thereof, from two years old and under, according to the time which he had diligently inquired of the wise men.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Here attend to me carefully.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because many things are uttered by many very idly touching these children, and the course of events is charged with injustice, and some of these express their perplexity about it in a more moderate way, others with more of audaciousness and frenzy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In order then that we may free these of their madness and those of their perplexity, suffer us to discourse a little upon this topic.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Plainly, then, if this be their charge, that the children were left to be slain, they should find fault likewise with the slaughter of the soldiers that kept Peter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as here, when the young child had fled, other children are massacred in the place of Him who was sought; even so then, too, Peter having been delivered from his prison and chains by the angel, one of like name with this tyrant, and like temper too, when he had sought him, and found him not, slew instead of him the soldiers that kept him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But what is this?” it may be said; “why this is not a solution, but an enhancement of our difficulty.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I know it too, and for this intent I bring forward all such cases, that to all I may adduce one and the same solution.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then is the solution of these things? or what fair account of them can we give?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That Christ was not the cause of their slaughter, but the king’s cruelty; as indeed neither was Peter to those others, but the madness of Herod.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if he had seen the wall broken through, or the doors overthrown, he might, perhaps, have had ground to accuse the soldiers that kept the apostle, of neglect; but now when all things continued in due form,and the doors were thrown wide open, and the chains fastened to the hands of them that kept him (for in fact they were bound unto him), he might have inferred from these things (that is, if he had been strictly doing a judge’s office on the matters before him), that the event was not of human power or craft, but of some divine and wonder-working power; he might have adored the doer of these things, instead of waging war with the sentinels.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For God had so done all that He did, that so far from exposing the keepers, He was by their means leading the king unto the truth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if he proved senseless, what signifies tothe skillful Physician of Souls, managing all things to do good, the insubordination of him that is diseased?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And just this one may say in the present case likewise.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, wherefore art thou wroth, O Herod, at being mocked of the wise men? didst thou not know that the birth was divine? didst thou not summon the chief priests? didst thou not gather together the scribes? did not they, being called, bring the prophet also with them into thy court of judgment, proclaiming these things beforehand from of old?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou not see how the old things agreed with the new?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou not hear that a star also ministered to these men?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou not reverence the zeal of the barbarians?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou not marvel at their boldness?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wast thou not horror-struck at the truth of the prophet?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou not from the former things perceive the very last also?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore didst thou not reason with thyself from all these things, that this event was not of the craft of the wise men, but of a Divine Power, duly dispensing all things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And even if thou wert deceived by the wise men, what is that tothe young children, who have done no wrong?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Yea,” saith one, “Herod thou hast full well deprived of excuse, and proved him blood-thirsty; but thou hast not yet solved the question about the injustice of what took place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if he did unjustly, wherefore did God permit it?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now, what should we say to this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That which I do not cease to say continually, in church, in the market-place and everywhere; that which I also wish you carefully to keep in mind, for it is a sort of rule for us, suited to every such perplexity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then is our rule, and what our saying?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That although there be many that injure, yet is there not so much as one that is injured.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in order that the riddle may not disturb you too much, I add the solution too with all speed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean, that what we may suffer unjustly from any one, it tells either to the doing away of our sins, God so putting that wrong to our account; or unto the recompense of rewards.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that what I may say may be clearer, let us conduct our argument in the way of illustration.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: As thus: suppose a certain servant who owes much money to his master, and then that this servant has been despitefully used by unjust men, and robbed of some of his goods.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If then the master, in whose power it was to stay the plunderer and wrong doer, should not indeed restore that same property, but should reckon what was taken away towards what was owed him by his servant, is the servant then injured?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By no means.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what if he should repay him even more?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Has he not then even gained more than he has lost?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Every one, I suppose, perceives it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now this same reckoning we are to make in regard of our own sufferings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to the fact, that in consideration of what we may suffer wrongfully, we either have sins done away, or receive more glorious crowns, if the amount of our sins be not so great: hear what Paul says concerning him that had committed fornication, “Deliver ye such a one to Satan for the destruction of the flesh, that the spirit may be saved.”“But what is this?” you may say, “for the discourse was about them that were injured by others, not about them that are corrected by their teachers.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I might answer, that there is no difference;for the question was, whether to suffer evil be not an indignity to the sufferer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But, to bring my argument nearer the very point inquired of; remember David, how, when he saw Shimei at a certain time assailing him, and trampling on his affliction, and pouring on him revilings without end, his captains desiring to slay him, he utterly forbade them, saying, “Let him curse, that the Lord may look upon mine abasement, and that he may requite me good for this cursing this day.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And in the Psalms too in his chanting, he said, “Consider mine enemies, that they are multiplied, and they hate me with unjust hatred,” and “forgive all my sins.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Lazarus again for the same cause enjoyed remission, having in this life suffered innumerable evils.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They therefore who are wronged, are not wronged if they bear nobly all that they suffer, yea, rather they gain even more abundantly, whether they be smitten of God, or scourged by the devil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But what kind of sin had these children,” it may be said, “that they should do it away? for touching those who are of full age, and have been guilty of many negligences, one might with show of reason speak thus: but they who so underwent premature death, what sort of sins did they by their sufferings put away?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Didst thou not hear me say, that though there were no sins, there is a recompense of rewards hereafter for them that suffer ill here?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherein then were the young children hurt in being slain for such a cause, and borne away speedily into that waveless harbor?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Because,” sayest thou, “they would in many instances have achieved, had they lived, many and great deeds of goodness.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, for this cause He lays up for them beforehand no small reward, the ending their lives for such a cause.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Besides, if the children were to have been any great persons, He would not have suffered them to be snatched away beforehand.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if they that eventually will live in continual wickedness are endured by Him with so great long-sufferings, much more would He not have suffered these to be so taken off had He foreknown they would accomplish any great things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And these are the reasons we have to give; yet these are not all; but there are also others more mysterious than these, which He knoweth perfectly, who Himself ordereth these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then give up unto Him the more perfect understanding of this matter, and apply ourselves to what follows, and in the calamities of others let us learn to bear all things nobly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, for it was no little scene of woe, which then befell Bethlehem, the children were snatched from their mother’s breast, and dragged unto this unjust slaughter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if thou art yet faint-hearted, and not equal to controlling thyself in these things, learn the end of him who dared all this, and recover thyself a little.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For very quickly was he overtaken by punishment for these things; and he paid the due penalty of such an abominable act, ending his life by a grievous death, and more pitiable than that which he now dared inflict;suffering also countless additional ills, which ye may know of by perusing Josephus’ account of these events.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But, lest we should make our discourse long, and interrupt its continuity, we have not thought it necessary to insert that account in what we are saying.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Then was fulfilled that which was spoken by Jeremy the prophet,saying, In Rama was there a voice heard, Rachel weeping for her children, and would not be comforted, because they are not.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus having filled the hearer with horror by relating these things: the slaughter so violent and unjust, so extremely cruel and lawless; he comforts him again, by saying, Not from God’s wanting power to prevent it did all this take place, nor from any ignorance of His, but when He both knew it, and foretold it,and that loudly by His prophet.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Be not troubled then, neither despond, looking unto His unspeakable providence, which one may most clearly see, alike by what He works, and by what He permits.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this He intimated in another place also, when discoursing to His disciples.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean where, having forewarned them of the judgment seats, and executions, and of the wars of the world, and of the battle that knows no truce, to uphold their spirit and to comfort them He saith, “Are not two sparrows sold for a farthing? and one of them shall not fall on the ground without your Father which is in Heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: These things He said, signifying that nothing is done without His knowledge, but while He knows all, yet not in all doth He act.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Be not then troubled,” He saith, “neither be disturbed.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if He know what ye suffer, and hath power to hinder it, it is quite clear that it is in His providence and care for you that He doth not hinder it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this we ought to bear in mind in our own temptations also, and great will be the consolation we shall thence receive.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what, it may be said, hath Rachel to do with Bethlehem?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For it saith, “Rachel weeping for her children.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what hath Rama to do with Rachel?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Rachel was the mother of Benjamin, and on his death, they buried her in the horse-course that was near this place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The tomb then being near, and the portion pertaining unto Benjamin her infant (for Rama was of the tribe of Benjamin), from the head of the tribe first, and next from the place of her sepulture, He naturally denominates her young children who were massacred.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then to show that the wound that befell her was incurable and cruel, He saith, “she would not be comforted because they are not.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Hence again we are taught this, which I mentioned before, never to be confounded when what is happening is contrary to the promise of God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Behold, for instance, when He was come for the salvation of the people, or rather for the salvation of the world, of what kind were His beginnings.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: His mother, first, in flight; His birth-place is involved in irremediable calamities, and a murder is perpetrated of all murders the bitterest, and there is lamentation and great mourning, and wailings everywhere.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But be not troubled; for He is wont ever to accomplish His own dispensations by their contraries, affording us from thence a very great demonstration of His power.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus did He lead on His own disciples also, and prepared them to do all their duty, bringing about things by their contraries, that the marvel might be greater.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: They, at any rate, being scourged and persecuted, and suffering terrors without end, did in this way get the better of them that were beating and persecuting them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But when Herod was dead, behold, an angel of the Lord appeareth in a dream to Joseph saying, Arise, and take the young Child and His mother, and go into the land of Israel.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He no more saith “fly,” but “go.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou again after the temptation refreshment? then after the refreshment danger again? in that he was freed indeed from his banishment, and came back again to his own country; and beheld the murderer of the children brought to the slaughter;but when he hath set foot on his own country, he finds again a remnant of the former perils, the son of the tyrant living, and being king.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But how did Archelaus reign over Judæa, when Pontius Pilate was governor?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Herod’s death had recently taken place, and the kingdom had not yet been divided into many parts; but as he had only just ended his life, the son for a while kept possession of the kingdom “in the room of his father Herod;” his brother also bearing this name, which is the reason why the evangelist added, “in the room of his father Herod.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It may be said, however, “if he was afraid to settle in Judæa on account of Archelaus, he had cause to fear Galilee also on account of Herod.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I answer, By his changing the place, the whole matter was thenceforward thrown into shade; for the whole assault was upon “Bethlehem and the coasts thereof.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore now that the slaughter had taken place, the youth Archelaus had no other thought, but that the whole was come to an end, and that amongst the many, He that was sought had been destroyed.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And besides, his father having come to such an end of his life before his eyes, he became for the future more cautious about farther proceedings, and about urging on that course of iniquity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Joseph therefore comes to Nazareth, partly to avoid the danger, partly also delighting to abide in his native place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To give him the more courage, he receives also an oracle from the angel touching this matter.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Luke, however, doth not say that he came there by Divine warning, but that when they had fulfilled all the purification, they returned to Nazareth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then may one say?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That Luke is giving an account of the time before the going down to Egypt, when he saith these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He would not have brought them down thither before the purification, in order that nothing should be done contrary to the law, but he waited for her to be purified, and to go to Nazareth, and that then they should go down to Egypt.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, after their return, He bids them go to Nazareth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But before this they were not warned of God to go thither, but yearning after their native place, they did so of their own accord.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For since they had gone up for no other cause but on account of the taxing, and had not so much as a place where to stay, when they had fulfilled that for which they had come up, they went down to Nazareth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: We see here the cause why the angel also, putting them at ease for the future, restores them to their home.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And not even this simply, but he adds to it a prophecy, “That it might be fulfilled,” saith he, “which was spoken by the prophets, He shall be called a Nazarene.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what manner of prophet said this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Be not curious, nor overbusy.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For many of the prophetic writings have been lost; and this one may see from the history of the Chronicles.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For being negligent, and continually falling into ungodliness, some they suffered to perish, others they themselves burnt upand cut to pieces.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The latter fact Jeremiah relates;the former, he who composed the fourth book of Kings, saying, that aftera long time the book of Deuteronomy was hardly found, buried somewhere and lost.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if, when there was no barbarian there, they so betrayed their books, much more when the barbarians had overrun them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to the fact, that the prophet had foretold it, the apostles themselves in many places call Him a Nazarene.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Was not this then,” one may say, “casting a shade over the prophecy touching Bethlehem?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By no means: rather this very fact was sure greatly to stir up men, and to awaken them to the search of what was said of Him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus, for example, Nathanael too enters on the inquiry concerning Him, saying, “Can there any good thing come out of Nazareth?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the place was of little esteem; or rather not that place only, but also the whole district of Galilee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore the Pharisees said, “Search and look, for out of Galilee ariseth no prophet.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, He is not ashamed to be named even from thence, signifying that He needs not ought of the things of men; and His disciples also He choses out of Galilee; everywhere cutting off the pretexts of them who are disposed to be remiss, and giving tokens that we have no need of outward things, if we practise virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this cause He doth not choose for Himself so much as a house; for “the Son of Man,” saith He, “hath not where to lay His head;”and when Herod is plotting against Him, He fleeth, and at His birth is laid in a manger, and abides in an inn, and takes a mother of low estate; teaching us to think no such thing a disgrace, and from the first outset trampling under foot the haughtiness of man, and bidding us give ourselves up to virtue only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For why dost thou pride thyself on thy country, when I am commanding thee to be a stranger to the whole world? (so He speaks); when thou hast leave to become such as that all the universe shall not be worthy of thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For these things are so utterly contemptible, that they are not thought worthy of any consideration even amongst the philosophers of the Greeks, but are called Externals, and occupy the lowest place.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But yet Paul,” one may say, “allows them, saying on this wise, ‘As touching the election, they are beloved for the fathers’ sake.’”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But tell me, when, and of what things was he discoursing, and to whom?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, to those of Gentile origin, who were puffing themselves up on their faith, and exalting themselves against the Jews, and so breaking them off the more: to quell the swelling pride of the one, and to win over the others, and thoroughly excite them to the same emulation.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For when he is speaking of those noble and great men, hear how he saith, “They that say these things, show plainly that they seek a country; and truly if they had been mindful of that from whence they came out, they might have had opportunity to have returned: but now they desire another, a better country.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And again, “These all died in faith, not having obtained the promises, but having seen them afar off, and embraced them.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And John too said unto those that were coming to him, “Think not to say, We have Abraham to our father.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And Paul again, “For they are not all Israel, which are of Israel; neither they, which are the children of the flesh, are they the children of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what were the sons of Samuel advantaged, tell me, by their father’s nobleness, when they were not heirs of their father’s virtue?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what profit had Moses’ sons, not having emulated his perfection?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Therefore neither did they inherit the dominion; but whilst they enrolled him as their father, the rule of the people passed away to another, to him who had become his son in the way of virtue.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what harm was it to Timothy, that he was of a Greek father?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or what on the other hand again was Noah’s son profited by the virtue of his father, when he became a slave instead of free?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou, how little the nobleness of a father avails his children in the way of advocacy?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the wickedness of Ham’s disposition overcame the laws of nature, and cast him not only out of the nobility which he had in respect of his father, but also out of his free estate.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what of Esau?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Was he not son of Isaac, and had he not his father to stand his friend?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, his father too endeavored and desired that he should partake of the blessings, and he himself for the sake of this did all that was commanded him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, because he was untoward,none of these things profited him; but although he was by birth first, and had his father on his side doing everything for this object, yet not having God with him, he lost all.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why do I speak of men?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The Jews were sons of God, and gained nothing by this their high birth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now if a man, having become a son of God, but failing to show forth an excellency meet for this noble birth, is even punished the more abundantly; why dost thou bring me forward the nobleness of ancestors remote or near?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not under the old covenantonly, but even under the new, one may find this rule to have held.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For “as many as received Him,” it is said “to them gave He power to become the sons of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And yet many of these children Paul hath affirmed to be nothing profited by their father; “For if ye be circumcised,” saith he, “Christ shall profit you nothing.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if Christ be no help to those who will not take heed to themselves, how shall a man stand up in their behalf?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us not therefore pride ourselves either on high birth, or on wealth, but rather despise them who are so minded: neither let us be dejected at poverty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But let us seek that wealth, which consists in good works; let us flee that poverty, which causes men to be in wickedness, by reason of which also that rich man was poor;wherefore he had not at his command so much as a drop of water, and that, although he made much entreaty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whereas, who can be so poor amongst us,as to want water enough even for comfort?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: There is none such.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even they that are pining with extreme hunger, may have the comfort of a drop of water; and not of a drop only, but of refreshment too far more abundant.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not so that rich man, but he was poor even to this degree: and what was yet more grievous, he could not so much as soothe his poverty from any source.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why then do we gape after riches, since they bring us not into Heaven?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For tell me, if any king among those upon earth had said, It is impossible for him that is rich to be distinguished at court, or to enjoy any honor; would ye not have thrown away every one his riches with contempt?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So then, if they cast us out from such honor as is in the palaces below, they shall be worthy of all contempt: but, when the King of Heaven is day by day crying aloud and saying, “It is hard with them, to set foot on that sacred threshold;” shall we not give up all, and withdraw from our possessions, that with boldness we may enter into the kingdom?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And of what consideration are we worthy, who are at great pains to encompass ourselves with the things that obstruct our way thither; and to hide them not only in chests, but even in the earth, when we might entrust them to the guard of the very Heavens?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since now surely thou art doing the same, as if any husbandman, having gotten wheat wherewith to sow a rich land, was to leave the land alone, and bury all the wheat in a pit, so as neither to enjoy it himself, nor for the wheat to come to ought, but decay and waste.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But what is their common plea, when we accuse them of these things?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: It gives no little comfort, say they, to know that all is laid up for us in safety at home.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, rather not to know of its being laid up is a comfort.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For even if thou art not afraid of famine, yet other more grievous things, on account of this store, must needs be a terror to thee: deaths, wars, plots laid against thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if a famine should ever befall us, the people again, constrained by the belly, takes weapon in hand against thy house.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or rather, in so doing, thou art first of all bringing famine into our cities, and next thou art forming for thine own house this gulf, more grievous than famine.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For by stress of famine I know not any who have come to a speedy end; there being in fact many means in many quarters which may be devised to assuage that evil: but for possessions and riches, and the pursuits connected with them, I can show many to have come by their ruin, some in secret, some openly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And with many such instances the highways abound, with many the courts of law, and the market-places.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But why speak I of the highways, the courts of law and the market-places?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, the very sea thou mayest behold filled with their blood.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not over the land only, as it seems, hath this tyranny prevailed, but over the ocean also hath walked in festal procession with great excess.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And one makes a voyage for gold, another, again, is stabbed for the same; and the same tyrannical power hath made one a merchant, the other a murderer.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then can be less trustworthy than Mammon, seeing that for his sake one travels, and ventures, and is slain?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But who,” it is said, “will pity a charmer that is bitten with a serpent?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For we ought, knowing its cruel tyranny, to flee that slavery, and destroy that grievous longing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But how,” saith one, “is this possible?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By introducing another longing, the longing for Heaven.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since he that desires the kingdom will laugh covetousness to scorn; he that is become Christ’s slave is no slave of mammon, but rather his lord; for him that flieth from him, he is wont to follow, and to fly from him that pursues.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He honors not so much his pursuer as his despiser; no one doth he so laugh to scorn, as them that desire him; nor doth he only laugh them to scorn, but wraps round them also innumerable bonds.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Be it ours then, however late, to loose these grievous chains.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why bring thy reasonable soul into bondage to brute matter, to the mother of those untold evils?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But, oh the absurdity! that while we are warring against it in words, it makes war with us by deeds, and leads and carries us everywhere about, insulting us as purchased with money, and meet for the lash; and what can be more disgraceful and dishonorable than this?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again: if we do not get the better of senseless forms of matter, how shall we have the advantage of the incorporeal powers?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If we despise not vile earth and abject stones, how shall we bring into subjection the principalities and authorities?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How shall we practise temperance?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I mean, if silver dazzle and overpower us, when shall we be able to hurry by a fair face?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For, in fact, some are so sold under this tyranny, as be moved somehow even at the mere show of the gold, and in playfulness to say, that the very eyes are the better for a gold coin coming in sight.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But make not such jests, whoever thou art;for nothing so injures the eyes, both those of the body and those of the soul, as the lust of these things.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For instance; it was this grievous longing that put out the lamps of those virgins, and cast them out of the bride chamber.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This sight, which (as thou saidst) “doeth good to the eyes,” suffered not the wretched Judas to hearken unto the Lord’s voice, but led him even to the halter, made him burst asunder in the midst; and, after all that, conducted him on to hell.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What then can be more lawless than this? what more horrible?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I do not mean the substance of riches, but the unseasonable and frantic desire of them?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Why, it even drops human gore, and looks murder, and is fiercer than any wild beast, tearing in pieces them that fall in its way, and what is much worse, it suffers them not even to have any sense of being so mangled.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For reason would that those who are so treated should stretch forth their hand to them that pass by, and call them to their assistance, but these are even thankful for such rendings of their flesh, than which what can be more wretched?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us then, bearing in mind all these things, flee the incurable disease; let us heal the wounds it hath made, and withdraw ourselves from such a pest: in order that both here we may live a secure and untroubled life, and attain to the future treasure; unto which God grant that we may all attain,by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, with whom unto the Father together with the Holy Ghost be glory, might, honor, now and ever, and world without end.
